title: Cicely
content:
آ آ آ آ Cicely says youï؟½re a poet; maybe, I ainï؟½t much on rhyme:
آ آ آ آ I reckon youï؟½d give me a hundred, and beat me every time.
آ آ آ آ Poetry! thatï؟½s the way some chaps puts up an idee,
آ آ آ آ But I takes mine ï؟½straight without sugar,ï؟½ and thatï؟½s whatï؟½s the matter with me.

آ آ آ آ Poetry! just look round you, alkali, rock, and sage;
آ آ آ آ Sage-brush, rock, and alkali; ainï؟½t it a pretty page!
آ آ آ آ Sun in the east at morninï؟½, sun in the west at night,
آ آ آ آ And the shadow of this ï؟½yer station the onï؟½y thing moves in sight.

آ آ آ آ Poetry! Well now Polly! Polly, run to your mam;
آ آ آ آ Run right away, my pooty! By-by! Ainï؟½t she a lamb?
آ آ آ آ Poetry! that reminds me oï؟½ suthinï؟½ right in that suit:
آ آ آ آ Jest shet that door thar, will yer? for Cicelyï؟½s ears is cute.

آ آ آ آ Ye noticed Polly, the baby? A month afore she was born,
آ آ آ آ Cicely my old woman was moody-like and forlorn;
آ آ آ آ Out of her head and crazy, and talked of flowers and trees;
آ آ آ آ Family man yourself, sir? Well, you know what a woman beï؟½s.

آ آ آ آ Narvous she was, and restless, said that she ï؟½couldnï؟½t stay.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Stay! and the nearest woman seventeen miles away.
آ آ آ آ But I fixed it up with the doctor, and he said he would be on hand,
آ آ آ آ And I kinder stuck by the shanty, and fenced in that bit oï؟½ land.

آ آ آ آ One night, the tenth of October, I woke with a chill and a fright,
آ آ آ آ For the door it was standing open, and Cicely warnï؟½t in sight,
آ آ آ آ But a note was pinned on the blanket, which it said that she ï؟½couldnï؟½t stay,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ But had gone to visit her neighbor, seventeen miles away!

آ آ آ آ When and how she stampeded, I didnï؟½t wait for to see,
آ آ آ آ For out in the road, next minit, I started as wild as she;
آ آ آ آ Running first this way and that way, like a hound that is off the scent,
آ آ آ آ For there warnï؟½t no track in the darkness to tell me the way she went.

آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve had some mighty mean moments afore I kem to this spot,
آ آ آ آ Lost on the Plains in ï؟½50, drownded almost and shot;
آ آ آ آ But out on this alkali desert, a-hunting a crazy wife,
آ آ آ آ Was raï؟½ly as on-satis-factory as anything in my life.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Cicely! Cicely! Cicely!ï؟½ I called, and I held my breath,
آ آ آ آ And ï؟½Cicely!ï؟½ came from the canyon, and all was as still as death.
آ آ آ آ And ï؟½Cicely! Cicely! Cicely!ï؟½ came from the rocks below,
آ آ آ آ And jest but a whisper of ï؟½Cicely!ï؟½ down from them peaks of snow.

آ آ آ آ I ainï؟½t what you call religious, but I jest looked up to the sky,
آ آ آ آ And this ï؟½yerï؟½s to what Iï؟½m coming, and maybe ye think I lie:
آ آ آ آ But up away to the eastï؟½ard, yaller and big and far,
آ آ آ آ I saw of a suddent rising the singlerist kind of star.

آ آ آ آ Big and yaller and dancing, it seemed to beckon to me:
آ آ آ آ Yaller and big and dancing, such as you never see:
آ آ آ آ Big and yaller and dancing, I never saw such a star,
آ آ آ آ And I thought of them sharps in the Bible, and I went for it then and thar.

آ آ آ آ Over the brush and bowlders I stumbled and pushed ahead,
آ آ آ آ Keeping the star afore me, I went wherever it led.
آ آ آ آ It might hev been for an hour, when suddent and peart and nigh,
آ آ آ آ Out of the yearth afore me thar riz up a babyï؟½s cry.

آ آ آ آ Listen! tharï؟½s the same music; but her lungs they are stronger now
آ آ آ آ Than the day I packed her and her mother, Iï؟½m derned if I jest know how.
آ آ آ آ But the doctor kem the next minit, and the joke oï؟½ the whole thing is
آ آ آ آ That Cis never knew what happened from that very night to this!

آ آ آ آ But Cicely says youï؟½re a poet, and maybe you might, some day,
آ آ آ آ Jest sling her a rhyme ï؟½bout a baby that was born in a curious way,
آ آ آ آ And see what she says; and, old fellow, when you speak of the star, donï؟½t tell
آ آ آ آ As how ï؟½twas the doctorï؟½s lantern, for maybe ï؟½twonï؟½t sound so well.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Bret Harte (Francis) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Bret Harte (Francis)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
